data.content_ref: inline:The claim asserts a violation of C13 (shared services) and C14 (historical intelligibility) based on an operator-experience failure mode, but the target's own counterconditions list four observation-evaluated conditions that would each independently negate the claimed failure. Countercondition 3 states 'Scratch authoring emits streaming events that are committed incrementally, not at task boundary'—if true, this directly contradicts the mechanism's core premise that 'the operator sees a plan committed to the ledger before any model output, then must wait for the bounded task to complete with no visibility into progress.' The target has not resolved whether streaming incremental commits exist in the actual implementation. Furthermore, the claim conflates two distinct concerns: (a) whether the deterministic fence is correct for replay (which the target concedes it is, satisfying C3), and (b) whether the fence creates poor UX (which is a design preference, not an architectural violation of C13/C14). C13 requires shared application services across CLI/MCP/chat—the target does not demonstrate that the attention fence prevents service sharing, only that it may create a specific interaction pattern some operators might dislike. C14 requires historical intelligibility—the target does not demonstrate that replay is unintelligible, only that it cannot reconstruct 'what the operator knew when,' which is an epistemic state that no deterministic replay system can capture without an explicit operator-intent log (which the target itself lists as a countercondition). The claim critiques a UX design choice as if it were an architectural violation, without resolving whether the factual premises about streaming behavior are even true in the codebase.
